body:
See the problem

How Slow Decisions Become Normal

Slow decisions rarely start with someone deliberately slowing things down. They usually begin with reasonable steps that keep adding people, checks and conversations.

Section 1 of 9 Approx. 8 min

Slow decisions often hide behind reasonable behaviour

At first, it looks responsible.

It can look like people doing the right thing.

  • Collaboration.
  • Inclusion.
  • Stakeholder management.
  • Risk awareness.
  • One more sensible conversation before something changes.

None of these are problems on their own.

The problem starts when they keep expanding without a clear path to a decision.

That is how reasonable behaviour becomes decision friction.

The Request Queue

An internal support team is receiving more requests than its current process is designed to handle.

Requests arrive from several channels

Email
“Can someone pick this up today?”
Teams
“Quick one — who can help with this request?”
Forwarded note
“Looping you in as discussed.”
Meeting follow-up
“Action from yesterday’s meeting.”
Direct request
“Can you help me with this?”
187
Overloaded queue

Requests pile up faster than the team can process them.

!
Some requests are urgent.
—
Some are not.
Some are duplicates.
•••
Some sit unanswered because everyone assumes someone else has picked them up.

The team has been asking for a proper workflow tool, but Technology says the earliest build window is two months away.

A simple workaround is proposed

While they wait for a proper workflow tool, the team lead has proposed a temporary workaround:

1

Shared inbox

One shared inbox for new requests.

2

Intake form

A short intake form.

3

Tracker

A shared tracker.

4

Triage check

A daily 15-minute triage check.

5

Priority rule

A clear rule for what gets prioritised.

Before making the change, the team lead has been asked to get feedback on the proposal.

Every stakeholder has a reasonable view

Most people agree the current process is not working.

But each group sees a different risk in changing it.

The team

Supports the idea because it would reduce chasing and duplication.

Technology

Is comfortable with a temporary workaround, provided it does not become a permanent shadow system.

Risk

Wants the tracker to capture enough information to show why urgent work was prioritised.

Business stakeholders

Worry the intake form will slow down urgent requests.

The team lead’s manager

Is concerned that daily triage will take people away from delivery work.

No one is trying to block the decision. Each concern is reasonable.

Two weeks later

  • The project lead has now discussed the proposal in three different meetings.
  • Each conversation has been constructive.
  • Each group has raised reasonable points.
“Let’s make sure the right people are comfortable before we change the process.”
  • Two weeks later, the proposal has more comments, more edits and more people copied into the discussion.
  • The current process is still in place.
  • The team is still chasing work through emails and side messages.
  • No one is obviously blocking the decision.

But the decision has not moved.

title: What is causing the stall?
body:
The proposal has support. It has conditions, concerns and comments. But it still has not moved.

What is most likely causing this decision to be stuck?
instruction: Select one option.

_items.text: The project lead has not clarified which stakeholders need to be involved before the decision can be made.
The concerns raised have not been fully addressed and communicated back to the stakeholders.
The team lead’s manager has not stepped in to close the discussion.
The decision owner is unclear, and advice is being treated like approval.

_feedback.correct:
That is the strongest diagnosis.

The central issue is not simply how much feedback has been gathered, or whether every comment has received a response. The team has provided input. Technology has given a condition. Risk has provided advice. Business stakeholders have raised a concern. The manager has raised a delivery trade-off.

But no one has clarified who can decide whether the temporary process should proceed, what advice must be addressed before that decision is made, and who simply needs to be informed.

When those roles stay blurred, reasonable consultation starts behaving like approval.

_incorrect.final:
That answer is plausible, but it does not get to the deeper issue.

The decision is not stuck because one stakeholder is blocking it or the right people have not been included. It is stuck because the decision roles are blurred.

Some people need to provide input. Some need to give advice or conditions. Some need to be informed. One person or forum needs to own the decision.

Without that role clarity, the discussion keeps expanding while the decision stays open.
_incorrect.notFinal:
That answer is plausible, but it does not get to the deeper issue.

The decision is not stuck because one stakeholder is blocking it or the right people have not been included. It is stuck because the decision roles are blurred.

Some people need to provide input. Some need to give advice or conditions. Some need to be informed. One person or forum needs to own the decision.

Without that role clarity, the discussion keeps expanding while the decision stays open.

_partlyCorrect.final:
That answer is plausible, but it does not get to the deeper issue.

The decision is not stuck because one stakeholder is blocking it or the right people have not been included. It is stuck because the decision roles are blurred.

Some people need to provide input. Some need to give advice or conditions. Some need to be informed. One person or forum needs to own the decision.

Without that role clarity, the discussion keeps expanding while the decision stays open.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
That answer is plausible, but it does not get to the deeper issue.

The decision is not stuck because one stakeholder is blocking it or the right people have not been included. It is stuck because the decision roles are blurred.

Some people need to provide input. Some need to give advice or conditions. Some need to be informed. One person or forum needs to own the decision.

Without that role clarity, the discussion keeps expanding while the decision stays open.

body:
Why this decision stayed open

The visible problem

The request queue.

The decision problem

Role clarity.

The team is not short of opinions. It is short of decision structure.

Without role clarity:

  • input starts to feel like approval;
  • advice starts to feel like veto;
  • concerns keep reopening the discussion;
  • comfort becomes the standard for moving forward;
  • no one is clear who can close the decision.

This is how slow decisions become normal. Not through one big failure, but through a series of reasonable delays that never quite resolve who owns the call.

This is where Velocity decision-making starts

Velocity is not about rushing decisions or bypassing the right checks.

It is a practical approach for helping decisions move faster by making ownership, roles, input, advice, escalation and closure clearer.

In the next section, we will introduce the Velocity method: what it asks people to pay attention to, and what it helps teams do differently, so decisions can move with pace, clarity and quality.

That starts with a few practical questions:

  • Who owns the decision?
  • Who is recommending a path forward?
  • Who needs to provide input?
  • Whose advice or agreement is required?
  • Who needs to be informed?
  • What would allow the decision to close and move to action?
Continue Back to module overview

title: From stuck to moving
body:
In The Request Queue, the visible problem was an overloaded request process.

The decision problem was different.

No one was clear who could close the decision, whose advice needed to be addressed, and who simply needed to be informed.

Velocity is designed to stop that kind of drag becoming normal.

title: What Velocity is
body:
Velocity is a practical decision-making method designed to help organisations move decisions from need to execution with less drag, better judgement and stronger follow-through.

It is not a rigid governance process.

It is a sequence of decision disciplines that can be used lightly for everyday decisions and more deliberately for higher-risk, cross-functional or strategic decisions.

title: The method at a glance
body:
Velocity helps decisions move by combining clear decision roles with practical disciplines that reduce drag from framing through follow-through.

Velocity has five connected stages:

  1. 1

    Frame the decision

    Clarify the choice, purpose, trade-offs and boundaries.

  2. 2

    Define the roles

    Clarify who owns, advises, agrees, decides and acts.

  3. 3

    Build confidence

    Gather the advice, evidence, agreement and challenge needed to decide.

  4. 4

    Close the decision

    Confirm what was decided, by whom, why, and what is now closed.

  5. 5

    Move it forward

    Turn the decision into action, follow-through and learning.

title: The method flexes
body:
The same five stages apply to most workplace decisions, but the amount of effort should flex.

A low-risk, reversible decision may only need a quick conversation.

A higher-risk, regulated, cross-functional or strategic decision may need clearer artefacts, stronger challenge and a more explicit record.

Velocity is not about adding process to every decision.

It is about using the right amount of structure for the decision in front of you.

Light use

For everyday decisions:

  • quick framing;
  • clear owner and decider;
  • targeted input;
  • verbal closure;
  • simple next action.

More deliberate use

For higher-risk decisions:

  • written decision brief;
  • explicit role map;
  • evidence and challenge;
  • decision record;
  • follow-up and learning loop.

title: Role clarity inside Velocity
body:
One stage of Velocity is Define the roles.

At IFM, Velocity uses Bain’s RAPID framework to help teams clarify who plays which role in a decision.

At IFM, RAPID is used within Velocity to help define decision roles. It is one part of the method, not the whole method.

RAPID helps define:

Recommend

Who recommends a path forward?

Agree

Who must agree before the decision proceeds?

Perform

Who performs the work?

Input

Who provides input?

Decide

Who decides?

Section 4 will go deeper into how these roles work in practice.

title: What the method helps teams do
displayTitle: What the method helps teams do
body:
Velocity helps teams turn the five stages into practical decision habits.

Frame

Start with the decision, not the discussion

Clarify what decision is actually being made before widening the conversation.

Roles

Define the roles needed for the decision to move

Make it clear who owns, advises, agrees, decides, acts and needs to know.

Confidence

Build enough confidence to decide responsibly

Gather the advice, evidence, agreement and challenge needed without letting consultation sprawl.

Closure

Close the decision clearly

Confirm what was decided, by whom, why, and what should no longer be reopened.

Action

Move the decision into action and learning

Turn the decision into follow-through, communication and learning.

These stages reduce common sources of decision drag: unclear ownership, expanding consultation, advice becoming approval, unresolved conditions, weak closure and decisions that do not translate into action.

title: Trust helps the method work
body:
Velocity also depends on trust.

People need enough trust to provide honest input, challenge constructively, surface conditions early and commit once a decision is closed.

But Velocity does not leave trust to chance.

It builds trust through clearer framing, clearer roles, better confidence-building, stronger closure and visible follow-through.

Future IFM connection: At IFM, this connects to iTRUST: the behaviours that support constructive challenge, confidence in the process and commitment after decisions are made.

title: Quick check
body: Which statement best describes Velocity?
instruction: Select one option.

_items.text: Velocity is mainly about making decisions faster by reducing consultation.
Velocity is a practical method that helps decisions move from framing through follow-through with the right amount of clarity, confidence and structure.
Velocity is another name for RAPID.
Velocity is mainly used when decisions need to be escalated.

_feedback.correct:
That is the best answer.

Velocity is designed to improve decision pace without lowering decision quality. It helps teams frame the decision, define roles, build confidence, close clearly and move the decision into action.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

Velocity is not just about speed, escalation or RAPID. It is a practical method for helping decisions move from need to execution with less drag, better judgement and stronger follow-through.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Velocity is not just about speed, escalation or RAPID. It is a practical method for helping decisions move from need to execution with less drag, better judgement and stronger follow-through.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

Velocity is not just about speed, escalation or RAPID. It is a practical method for helping decisions move from need to execution with less drag, better judgement and stronger follow-through.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Velocity is not just about speed, escalation or RAPID. It is a practical method for helping decisions move from need to execution with less drag, better judgement and stronger follow-through.

title: Next: start with the decision
body:
The first stage is to frame the decision clearly.

Before a team decides who needs to be involved, it needs to be clear what decision is actually being made, what sits inside the decision, and what boundaries matter.

The next section looks at how to start with the decision, not the discussion.

title: Start with the decision
body:
Stage 1: Frame the decision

The first stage of Velocity is to frame the decision.

This means clarifying the choice, purpose, boundaries and trade-offs before people jump into advice, options or solution-building.

Framing does not mean writing a business case for every decision.

It means making the decision clear enough that people know what they are contributing to.

title: When discussion hides the decision
body:
Many slow decisions begin as broad discussion topics.

They sound reasonable, but they do not yet make the decision clear.

“We need to improve the request process.”

What is unclear:

Are we diagnosing, choosing a solution, or approving a change?

“Let’s get feedback from the right people.”

What is unclear:

Feedback on what decision? For what purpose?

“We need a workflow tool.”

What is unclear:

Is the decision about the long-term tool, or what to do while waiting?

“People need to be involved so they support the change.”

What is unclear:

Are they shaping the decision, helping design the change, or being prepared for implementation?

title: The decision frame
body:
A useful decision frame answers six practical questions.

1

Decision question

What decision are we actually making?

2

Purpose

Why is this decision needed now?

3

Boundary

What is in scope and out of scope?

4

Constraints

What must be protected or complied with?

5

Trade-offs

What tensions need to be weighed?

6

Escalation trigger

What would make this decision need to go up?

title: Decision input or change engagement?
body:
Involvement matters, but not all involvement has the same purpose.

People are more likely to support change when they have been heard.

But not every engagement activity needs to keep the decision open.

Velocity asks: what kind of involvement is needed here?

Decision input

What it helps answer:

What do we need to know before deciding?

Does it keep the decision open?

Yes, if the input is decision-critical.

Required advice or agreement

What it helps answer:

What conditions must be resolved before closure?

Does it keep the decision open?

Yes, if required conditions are unresolved.

Change design input

What it helps answer:

How should the solution work in practice?

Does it keep the decision open?

Sometimes, if the design affects the decision.

Change engagement

What it helps answer:

Who needs to understand, prepare for or adopt the change?

Does it keep the decision open?

Not usually, unless it reveals a material decision issue.

Communication

What it helps answer:

What was decided and what happens next?

Does it keep the decision open?

No. This happens after closure.

Velocity does not reduce involvement. It clarifies the purpose of involvement.

If involvement could materially change the decision, bring it into the decision process.

If involvement is needed for adoption, plan it as part of change implementation.

title: Back to The Request Queue
body:
In The Request Queue, people were discussing the problem, the workaround, stakeholder concerns and technology constraints.

But the decision itself was not framed clearly enough.

Poor frame

“How do we fix the request process?”

Why it is weak:

Too broad. It could mean a new tool, a new team structure, new prioritisation rules, better reporting, or a temporary workaround.

Better frame

“Should we introduce a temporary request intake workaround for the next two months while we wait for the workflow tool build window?”

Why it is stronger:

It clarifies the choice, time horizon and boundary.

It separates the temporary process decision from the long-term technology decision.
instruction: Which frame is more useful for moving the decision forward?

_items.text: “How do we fix the request process?”
“Should we introduce a temporary request intake workaround for the next two months while we wait for the workflow tool build window?”

_feedback.correct: The second frame is stronger because it makes the decision specific. It does not solve everything. It clarifies the choice in front of the team.

_incorrect.final: The first frame names the broad problem, but it is too wide to move the decision. The team could end up discussing the long-term tool, the operating model, prioritisation rules or the temporary workaround all at once.
_incorrect.notFinal: The first frame names the broad problem, but it is too wide to move the decision. The team could end up discussing the long-term tool, the operating model, prioritisation rules or the temporary workaround all at once.

_partlyCorrect.final: The first frame names the broad problem, but it is too wide to move the decision. The team could end up discussing the long-term tool, the operating model, prioritisation rules or the temporary workaround all at once.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal: The first frame names the broad problem, but it is too wide to move the decision. The team could end up discussing the long-term tool, the operating model, prioritisation rules or the temporary workaround all at once.

title: What is in and out?
body:
A good decision frame also makes the boundary visible.

Without boundaries, people can unknowingly debate different decisions.

For the temporary workaround decision, which set of items is correctly inside the decision boundary?
instruction: Select the strongest classification.

_items.text: Inside: shared inbox, intake form, Risk tracker information, daily triage sustainability, stakeholder briefing. Outside: long-term workflow tool design, Technology roadmap reprioritisation, full future operating model.
Inside: long-term workflow tool design, Technology roadmap reprioritisation and the future operating model. Outside: the temporary shared inbox and intake form.
Inside: anything raised by a stakeholder. Outside: only items that Technology says are out of scope.
Inside: only the shared inbox and intake form. Outside: Risk information, triage sustainability and stakeholder briefing.

_feedback.correct:
Correct. The temporary workaround decision should include the shared inbox, intake form, tracker requirements, triage sustainability and stakeholder briefing.

It should not accidentally become the long-term technology decision or the future operating model decision. Those may matter, but they are not the same decision.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite. The temporary workaround decision should not accidentally become the long-term technology decision or the future operating model decision.

Those may matter, but they are not the same decision.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The temporary workaround decision should not accidentally become the long-term technology decision or the future operating model decision.

Those may matter, but they are not the same decision.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite. The temporary workaround decision should not accidentally become the long-term technology decision or the future operating model decision.

Those may matter, but they are not the same decision.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The temporary workaround decision should not accidentally become the long-term technology decision or the future operating model decision.

Those may matter, but they are not the same decision.

title: What would make this go up?
displayTitle: What would make this go up?
body:
Framing also helps clarify what can be decided locally and what would need to go up.

Escalation is not failure.

It is useful when a decision moves outside its agreed boundary.

The team lead can decide on a temporary intake process for the team.
instruction: But which of these would most likely require escalation?

_items.text: The team wants to trial a shared inbox for two months.
Risk asks for urgent requests to include a reason code in the tracker.
The workaround would require sensitive client information to be stored in an unapproved location.
A stakeholder dislikes using intake forms.

_feedback.correct:
C is the strongest answer.

The decision has moved beyond a simple team process change into a control, privacy or information-management issue. That is a boundary issue and may need escalation or specialist approval.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

Escalation is most useful when the decision crosses a boundary, such as authority, risk, value, precedent, privacy, regulation or enterprise impact. A concern or preference may need to be heard, but it does not automatically mean the decision must go up.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Escalation is most useful when the decision crosses a boundary, such as authority, risk, value, precedent, privacy, regulation or enterprise impact. A concern or preference may need to be heard, but it does not automatically mean the decision must go up.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

Escalation is most useful when the decision crosses a boundary, such as authority, risk, value, precedent, privacy, regulation or enterprise impact. A concern or preference may need to be heard, but it does not automatically mean the decision must go up.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Escalation is most useful when the decision crosses a boundary, such as authority, risk, value, precedent, privacy, regulation or enterprise impact. A concern or preference may need to be heard, but it does not automatically mean the decision must go up.

title: Quick check and transition
body: Framing clarifies the decision before people start assigning roles, gathering input or debating options.
instruction: Which statement best describes why framing matters?

_items.text: It proves the preferred solution before people are consulted.
It makes the decision clear enough that people know what they are contributing to.
It replaces the need for decision roles.
It makes every decision more formal.

_feedback.correct:
That is the best answer.

Framing clarifies the decision before people start assigning roles, gathering input or debating options.

Once the decision is framed, the next question is who needs to play which role.

The next section introduces Define the roles: how Velocity uses role clarity to reduce drift, hidden vetoes and default escalation.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

Framing is not about proving the answer or adding formality. It is about making the decision clear enough for the right people to play the right roles.

Once the decision is framed, the next question is who needs to play which role.

The next section introduces Define the roles: how Velocity uses role clarity to reduce drift, hidden vetoes and default escalation.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Framing is not about proving the answer or adding formality. It is about making the decision clear enough for the right people to play the right roles.

Once the decision is framed, the next question is who needs to play which role.

The next section introduces Define the roles: how Velocity uses role clarity to reduce drift, hidden vetoes and default escalation.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

Framing is not about proving the answer or adding formality. It is about making the decision clear enough for the right people to play the right roles.

Once the decision is framed, the next question is who needs to play which role.

The next section introduces Define the roles: how Velocity uses role clarity to reduce drift, hidden vetoes and default escalation.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Framing is not about proving the answer or adding formality. It is about making the decision clear enough for the right people to play the right roles.

Once the decision is framed, the next question is who needs to play which role.

The next section introduces Define the roles: how Velocity uses role clarity to reduce drift, hidden vetoes and default escalation.

title: Once the decision is framed
body:
Once a decision is framed, the next question is not simply, “Who should be invited?”

It is:

Who needs to play which role?

A decision can involve many people, but not everyone is involved in the same way.

If the roles are unclear, consultation expands, advice can behave like approval, and decisions stay open longer than they need to.

title: RAPID is more than five letters
displayTitle: RAPID is more than five letters
body:
At IFM, Velocity uses Bain’s RAPID framework to define core decision roles.

RAPID helps teams clarify who:

  • recommends a path forward;
  • agrees before the decision proceeds;
  • performs the work;
  • provides input;
  • decides.

RAPID helps define who recommends, agrees, performs, inputs and decides. Velocity uses that clarity as one part of a broader method for moving decisions from framing through follow-through.

But the value of RAPID is not the acronym.

The value is the discipline behind the roles.

RAPID helps make clear what each person is responsible for — and what they are not responsible for.

title: The five RAPID roles
body:
RAPID is useful when the role rules are practical, not just labelled.

R

Recommend

Develops the recommendation or preferred path.

Usually gathers input, works through advice, shapes options and brings a clear proposal to the Decider.

A

Agree

Has a required agreement right before the decision proceeds.

Use this sparingly. Agree should usually be reserved for genuine control, policy, risk, compliance, authority or approval requirements.

P

Perform

Executes or implements the decision once it is made.

Perform should be involved early enough to test feasibility, but Perform does not own the decision just because they carry it out.

I

Input

Provides information, expertise, context or perspective.

Input is considered by the Recommender and Decider, but it is not approval or veto by default.

D

Decide

Makes the final decision and owns the trade-off.

There should usually be one clear Decider. If there appear to be multiple Deciders, the decision may need to be reframed, split or escalated.

title: What the roles do not mean
displayTitle: What the roles do not mean
body:
Role clarity also means knowing what each role does not mean.

Recommend does not mean Decide

The Recommender shapes the proposal. They do not automatically have final authority.

Agree does not mean “everyone who should be comfortable”

Agree should be used for required agreement rights, not general stakeholder support.

Perform does not mean “responsible for the whole decision”

Perform owns execution. They should be heard early, but they do not automatically own the decision process.

Input does not mean approval

Input should inform the decision. It should not block the decision unless that person or group also has a defined Agree or approval role.

Decide does not mean “the most senior person in the room”

The Decider should be the person or forum with the right authority for the decision, not just the highest-ranking attendee.

When the role rules are unclear, RAPID becomes decoration. The letters look tidy, but the decision still drifts.

title: Who keeps the decision moving?
body:
A common question is:

Who is responsible for moving the decision through the Velocity stages?

In many decisions, the Recommender does much of the work to prepare the decision.

The Recommender may gather input, work through advice, shape options and bring a recommendation to the Decider.

Perform is different.

Perform is responsible for carrying out the decision once it is made. Perform should be involved early enough to test feasibility, but Perform is not usually responsible for driving the decision process.

Velocity also asks teams to be explicit about the decision owner or process lead.

That is the person responsible for keeping the decision moving through the process.

Often this will be the Recommender, but it should still be named.

Recommender

Shapes the recommendation and often coordinates decision preparation.

Performer

Executes the decision and provides feasibility input.

Decision owner / process lead

Keeps the decision moving through the Velocity stages.

Decision owner / process lead is not an extra RAPID letter. It is a Velocity process clarity requirement that often sits with the Recommender.

title: Advice, agreement or approval?
body:
One of the most common role problems is confusing advice, agreement and approval.

These are not the same thing.

Advice

A view, risk assessment, recommendation or expert perspective that informs the decision.

Advice should be considered, but it does not automatically stop the decision.

Agreement

A required role that must be satisfied before the decision proceeds.

Agreement should be used carefully and only where there is a genuine condition, control or agreement right.

Approval

Formal authority to permit or reject something, usually because of delegated authority, policy, control or governance requirements.

Approval should be explicit. It should not hide inside general consultation.

If advice is treated like approval, decisions wait for comfort instead of clarity.

Compare your thinking

Mini-scenario: Risk identifies two controls that need to be in place before the temporary workaround proceeds.

What role is Risk playing?

It depends on the governance setting.

If Risk has a required control or agreement role, those conditions must be resolved before closure.

If Risk is advising only, the Decider must consider the advice and decide whether the residual risk is acceptable.

The important thing is to make the role explicit.

title: When someone can stop the decision
displayTitle: When someone can stop the decision
body:
Some people or functions may be able to stop a decision.

That may be legitimate when they hold delegated authority, policy, regulatory, risk, compliance, control, security or legal approval rights.

The problem is when this authority is hidden.

Velocity asks teams to make that authority explicit.

If someone can genuinely stop the decision, clarify whether they are:

  • an Agree role;
  • a formal approver;
  • a control owner;
  • a required escalation point;
  • or simply an Input role whose concerns need to be considered.

Do not hide veto power inside Input.

Scenario

Technology is comfortable with a temporary workaround unless it creates architecture, privacy or security risk outside agreed thresholds.

What does this mean?

Technology may be an Input or advice role for normal process impacts.

If the workaround crosses architecture, privacy or security thresholds, Technology may have a required agreement, approval or escalation role.

The threshold should be named, not discovered late.

title: One decision, one Decider
body:
A useful RAPID rule is that a decision should usually have one clear Decider.

If there appear to be multiple Deciders, one of three things may be happening:

  1. the decision has not been framed clearly enough;
  2. the decision needs to be split into smaller decisions;
  3. the authority boundary needs to be escalated or clarified.

A committee can be the Decider only if its decision mechanism is clear.

Majority vote

Chair decides after discussion

Consensus required

Delegated authority sits with a specific role

If no one can name the Decider, the decision is not ready to move.
instruction: Which situation most likely signals a problem?

_items.text: One person recommends and another decides.
Input providers disagree with the recommendation.
Two leaders both believe they have the final say on the same decision.
The Performer raises a feasibility concern before closure.

_feedback.correct: C is the strongest signal of role confusion. If two people both appear to have the final say, the decision may need to be reframed, split or escalated.

_incorrect.final: Not quite. The strongest signal of role confusion is when two people both appear to have the final say on the same decision. In that case, the decision may need to be reframed, split or escalated.
_incorrect.notFinal: Not quite. The strongest signal of role confusion is when two people both appear to have the final say on the same decision. In that case, the decision may need to be reframed, split or escalated.

_partlyCorrect.final: Not quite. The strongest signal of role confusion is when two people both appear to have the final say on the same decision. In that case, the decision may need to be reframed, split or escalated.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal: Not quite. The strongest signal of role confusion is when two people both appear to have the final say on the same decision. In that case, the decision may need to be reframed, split or escalated.

title: Who communicates and who acts?
body:
RAPID helps clarify decision roles, but communication ownership still needs to be named.

A useful rule of thumb:

Decider

Owns the decision and the trade-off.

Recommender

Often prepares the decision recap or rationale.

Performer

Usually communicates what changes in execution.

Someone still needs to inform

The people affected need to know what changed and what happens next.

The key is not to assume communication will happen because the decision was made.

Communication should be assigned.

title: Apply it to The Request Queue
displayTitle: Apply it to The Request Queue
body:
In The Request Queue, the team had useful input, advice and concerns.

What it did not have was clear role definition.

Compare your thinking: Review the likely role mapping below. The exact answer depends on authority and governance, but this shows the kind of clarification Velocity is trying to create.

Team lead

Likely decision owner / Recommender.

They are shaping the temporary workaround and keeping the decision moving.

Team members

Input and Perform.

They provide operational insight and will likely carry out the new process.

Technology

Input or advice for normal process impacts.

Agree, approval or escalation role only if the workaround crosses architecture, security, privacy or system thresholds.

Risk

Advice or Agree, depending on control requirements.

If Risk has required conditions, they need to be resolved before closure.

Business stakeholders

Input on urgency and user impact.

May need to be engaged or informed if the workaround proceeds, but they do not automatically approve the decision.

Team lead’s manager

Possible Decider, sponsor or escalation point depending on authority.

This must be clarified.

Business sponsor

Possible Decider if the decision sits above the team lead or manager’s authority.

Again, this must be explicit.

This is why role clarity matters.

The point is not to give everyone a letter.

The point is to clarify who is shaping the recommendation, who has required agreement rights, who provides input, who decides, who performs, and who needs to know.

Without that clarity, reasonable participation can start behaving like approval.

title: Quick check and transition
body: Once the decision is framed, role clarity helps people understand what they are responsible for, what authority they have, and how their contribution affects the decision.
instruction: Which statement best describes role clarity in Velocity?

_items.text: It means every stakeholder gets a formal role in the decision.
It means the most senior person automatically becomes the Decider.
It means people know whether they are recommending, agreeing, performing, providing input, deciding, acting or being informed.
It means RAPID replaces the need to frame the decision.

_feedback.correct:
That is the best answer.

Role clarity helps people understand what they are responsible for, what authority they have, and how their contribution affects the decision.

Once the roles are clear, the next question is what needs to be resolved before the decision can responsibly close.

The next section introduces Build confidence: how to gather the advice, evidence, agreement and challenge needed to decide without letting the process sprawl.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

Role clarity is not about giving everyone a formal role or defaulting to hierarchy. It is about making each person’s role in the decision explicit enough to avoid drift, hidden vetoes and unnecessary escalation.

Once the roles are clear, the next question is what needs to be resolved before the decision can responsibly close.

The next section introduces Build confidence: how to gather the advice, evidence, agreement and challenge needed to decide without letting the process sprawl.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Role clarity is not about giving everyone a formal role or defaulting to hierarchy. It is about making each person’s role in the decision explicit enough to avoid drift, hidden vetoes and unnecessary escalation.

Once the roles are clear, the next question is what needs to be resolved before the decision can responsibly close.

The next section introduces Build confidence: how to gather the advice, evidence, agreement and challenge needed to decide without letting the process sprawl.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

Role clarity is not about giving everyone a formal role or defaulting to hierarchy. It is about making each person’s role in the decision explicit enough to avoid drift, hidden vetoes and unnecessary escalation.

Once the roles are clear, the next question is what needs to be resolved before the decision can responsibly close.

The next section introduces Build confidence: how to gather the advice, evidence, agreement and challenge needed to decide without letting the process sprawl.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Role clarity is not about giving everyone a formal role or defaulting to hierarchy. It is about making each person’s role in the decision explicit enough to avoid drift, hidden vetoes and unnecessary escalation.

Once the roles are clear, the next question is what needs to be resolved before the decision can responsibly close.

The next section introduces Build confidence: how to gather the advice, evidence, agreement and challenge needed to decide without letting the process sprawl.

title: Once the roles are clear
body:
Once the decision is framed and the roles are clear, the next question is not:

Has everyone agreed?

It is:

What must be resolved before we can decide responsibly?

That is the purpose of Build confidence.

title: Confidence is not consensus
body:
Build confidence does not mean removing all uncertainty.

It does not mean...

  • everyone agrees;
  • everyone feels comfortable;
  • all uncertainty has been removed;
  • the decision is guaranteed to work.

It means...

The decision-maker has enough advice, evidence, agreement and challenge to decide responsibly for the level of risk involved.

Build confidence means gathering enough decision-relevant advice, evidence, agreement and challenge to decide responsibly — not removing all uncertainty or waiting until everyone is comfortable.

title: What builds confidence?
body:
Confidence comes from resolving the things that matter for this decision.

Advice

Expert or stakeholder view

What expert or stakeholder view needs to be considered?

Evidence

Decision-useful facts

What facts, options, assumptions or data matter enough to decide?

Agreement

Required conditions

What required conditions must be resolved before closure?

Challenge

Risks and blind spots

What risks, trade-offs or blind spots need to be tested?

Feasibility

Can this be acted on?

Can the decision be acted on in practice?

title: Enough for this decision
body:
Not every decision needs the same level of confidence-building.

A low-risk, reversible decision may need a short conversation and a clear owner.

A higher-risk, regulated, cross-functional or hard-to-reverse decision may need stronger evidence, clearer agreement conditions, more deliberate challenge and a better record.

The question is not:

Have we done everything?

The question is:

Have we done enough for this decision?

Light confidence-building

For low-risk or reversible decisions:

  • quick advice;
  • clear owner and decider;
  • limited input;
  • verbal closure;
  • simple next action.

More deliberate confidence-building

For higher-risk, regulated, cross-functional or hard-to-reverse decisions:

  • targeted advice;
  • stronger evidence;
  • explicit agreement conditions;
  • challenge or risk review;
  • clearer record and follow-up.

title: The confidence traps
body:
Confidence-building creates drag when it becomes open-ended.

Broad consultation

What it sounds like:

“Let’s get a few more views.”

What to ask instead:

Whose input would change the decision?

Endless evidence

What it sounds like:

“Can we get more data?”

What to ask instead:

What evidence would be enough?

Comfort-seeking

What it sounds like:

“Let’s make sure everyone is comfortable.”

What to ask instead:

Which unresolved condition prevents closure?

Hidden dissent

What it sounds like:

“Looks fine.”

What to ask instead:

What concern is not being said in the room?

Risk delay

What it sounds like:

“Risk still has concerns.”

What to ask instead:

Are these required conditions or advice to weigh?

Agreement sprawl

What it sounds like:

“Everyone needs to sign off.”

What to ask instead:

Who genuinely has agreement rights?

The point is not that these concerns are wrong. The point is that the team needs to clarify what would be enough to decide.

title: Back to The Request Queue
body:
In The Request Queue, the team did need confidence before deciding.

But not every concern needed to keep the decision open.

The temporary workaround affects the team’s daily process, stakeholder request behaviour, technology boundaries and risk controls.

Before deciding, the team needs enough confidence that the workaround is workable, safe enough and worth trialling.

Risk condition

Tracker requirements

What information must the tracker capture for urgent work?

Technology boundary

Shadow system risk

What would stop the workaround becoming a permanent shadow system?

Operational feasibility

Daily triage load

What operational load would daily triage create?

User behaviour

Bypassed requests

What would happen if an urgent request bypasses the intake form?

Review point

Pause, change or escalate

What would make the trial pause, change or escalate?

title: What would actually help?
body:
Which actions would most help the team build enough confidence to decide?

A. Ask every business stakeholder to review the intake form before the trial starts.

B. Ask Risk to define the minimum information needed in the tracker for urgent work.

C. Ask Technology to confirm what would make the workaround an unacceptable shadow system.

D. Ask the team to estimate the operational impact of a daily 15-minute triage.

E. Delay the decision until the workflow tool build window opens.

F. Run a short challenge discussion: what could make this workaround fail in the first month?
instruction: Select the strongest set of actions.

_items.text: A, B, C and E
B, C, D and F
A, D, E and F
A, B, C, D, E and F

_feedback.correct:
Correct. The strongest actions are the ones that answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, technology boundaries, operational feasibility and early failure points.

Broad stakeholder review may help adoption later, but it should not automatically keep the decision open.

Waiting for the long-term tool avoids the interim decision rather than improving it.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite. The strongest actions are B, C, D and F because they answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, technology boundaries, operational feasibility and early failure points.

Broad stakeholder review may help adoption later, but it should not automatically keep the decision open.

Waiting for the long-term tool avoids the interim decision rather than improving it.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The strongest actions are B, C, D and F because they answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, technology boundaries, operational feasibility and early failure points.

Broad stakeholder review may help adoption later, but it should not automatically keep the decision open.

Waiting for the long-term tool avoids the interim decision rather than improving it.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite. The strongest actions are B, C, D and F because they answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, technology boundaries, operational feasibility and early failure points.

Broad stakeholder review may help adoption later, but it should not automatically keep the decision open.

Waiting for the long-term tool avoids the interim decision rather than improving it.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The strongest actions are B, C, D and F because they answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, technology boundaries, operational feasibility and early failure points.

Broad stakeholder review may help adoption later, but it should not automatically keep the decision open.

Waiting for the long-term tool avoids the interim decision rather than improving it.

title: Quick check and transition
body: Build confidence is the discipline of resolving what matters enough to decide responsibly.
instruction: Which statement best describes Build confidence?

_items.text: It means continuing consultation until everyone is comfortable.
It means gathering enough advice, evidence, agreement and challenge to decide responsibly.
It means proving the decision will definitely work.
It means Risk or senior leaders decide whether the team can proceed.

_feedback.correct:
That is the best answer.

Build confidence means resolving what matters enough to decide responsibly.

It does not mean certainty, consensus or universal comfort.

Once the decision-maker has enough confidence to decide responsibly, the next risk is false progress.

A decision can be discussed, supported and even “agreed” without being properly closed.

The next section introduces Close the decision.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

Confidence is not certainty or comfort.

It is enough decision-relevant advice, evidence, agreement and challenge for the level of risk involved.

Once the decision-maker has enough confidence to decide responsibly, the next risk is false progress.

A decision can be discussed, supported and even “agreed” without being properly closed.

The next section introduces Close the decision.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Confidence is not certainty or comfort.

It is enough decision-relevant advice, evidence, agreement and challenge for the level of risk involved.

Once the decision-maker has enough confidence to decide responsibly, the next risk is false progress.

A decision can be discussed, supported and even “agreed” without being properly closed.

The next section introduces Close the decision.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

Confidence is not certainty or comfort.

It is enough decision-relevant advice, evidence, agreement and challenge for the level of risk involved.

Once the decision-maker has enough confidence to decide responsibly, the next risk is false progress.

A decision can be discussed, supported and even “agreed” without being properly closed.

The next section introduces Close the decision.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Confidence is not certainty or comfort.

It is enough decision-relevant advice, evidence, agreement and challenge for the level of risk involved.

Once the decision-maker has enough confidence to decide responsibly, the next risk is false progress.

A decision can be discussed, supported and even “agreed” without being properly closed.

The next section introduces Close the decision.

title: From confidence to closure
body:
Once the decision-maker has enough confidence to decide responsibly, the next risk is false progress.

A decision can be discussed.

It can be supported.

It can even feel agreed.

But unless it is clearly closed, people may still leave with different assumptions about what happens next.

That is the purpose of Close the decision.

title: The false progress problem
body:
False progress happens when a group leaves a conversation feeling aligned, but without a clear decision.

It often sounds like:

“This feels like the right direction.”

What may still be unclear:

Is this a decision or just support for more work?

“Let’s keep progressing.”

What may still be unclear:

What exactly should progress?

“We’re broadly aligned.”

What may still be unclear:

Who has decided, and what has been decided?

“Let’s socialise this once more.”

What may still be unclear:

Is input still needed, or is this communication?

“We’ll pick this up in the next forum.”

What may still be unclear:

Is the decision moving forward or being reopened?

These phrases are not wrong. But they do not always close the decision.

title: What closure means
body:
Closing a decision means making it clear enough to act on.

A closed decision should answer:

1

What was decided?

2

Who made the decision?

3

Why was this decision made?

4

What trade-offs, risks or conditions were accepted?

5

What remains open?

6

What is now closed?

7

Who needs to know?

8

What happens next?

A decision is not closed because people had a good discussion or broadly agreed.

A decision is closed when the decision is explicitly confirmed, recorded at the right level, communicated to the right people, and protected from casual reopening.

title: Closed does not mean locked forever
displayTitle: Closed does not mean locked forever
body:
Closing a decision does not mean pretending nothing could change.

Some decisions should have review points.

Some should be revisited if new information emerges.

The point is to make reopening intentional, not casual.

A decision may need to be reopened when:

A material assumption changes

A risk or control condition is not met

Implementation shows the decision is not workable

The context changes enough to affect the original rationale

An agreed review point is reached

A closed decision can still have review points. It should not have endless informal reopening.

Closure protects the decision from being quietly re-litigated every time someone feels uncertain.

title: The decision recap
body:
A simple decision recap can close most everyday decisions.

It does not need to be long.

It just needs to make the decision clear.

Decision

What exactly have we decided?

Decider

Who made the decision?

Rationale

Why this option?

Conditions

What conditions or controls must be met?

Trade-offs

What did we accept or prioritise?

Open items

What is still undecided?

Communication

Who needs to know?

Next step

What happens next, and who owns it?

Reopen trigger

What would justify revisiting the decision?

This is a lightweight recap, not a formal governance document. No ceremonial robes required.

title: Back to The Request Queue
body:
In The Request Queue, the team could have reduced drift by closing the temporary workaround decision clearly.

Here is what closure might sound like.

Example closure statement

The decision is to trial the temporary request intake workaround for two months.

The trial will include:

  • one shared inbox;
  • a short intake form;
  • a shared tracker;
  • a daily 15-minute triage check;
  • a prioritisation rule for urgent requests.

The team lead’s manager has made the decision, based on the recommendation from the team lead and input from Technology, Risk, business stakeholders and team members.

The long-term workflow tool decision remains separate.

Risk’s minimum tracker requirements will be included.

Technology’s condition is that the workaround must not become a permanent shadow system or store information in an unapproved location.

The decision will be reviewed after four weeks, or earlier if urgent requests are being delayed, control conditions are not being met, or the triage process is creating unsustainable workload.
instruction: Which part of this statement most helps prevent the decision being reopened later?

_items.text: It says the team supports the workaround.
It separates the temporary workaround from the long-term workflow tool decision.
It says several stakeholders were consulted.
It refers to the current process being difficult.

_feedback.correct:
The strongest answer is B.

By separating the temporary workaround from the long-term workflow tool decision, the team prevents one decision from being reopened through a different, larger decision.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

The useful closure move is separating the temporary workaround decision from the long-term workflow tool decision. Without that boundary, the team can keep reopening the temporary decision by debating the larger technology decision.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

The useful closure move is separating the temporary workaround decision from the long-term workflow tool decision. Without that boundary, the team can keep reopening the temporary decision by debating the larger technology decision.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

The useful closure move is separating the temporary workaround decision from the long-term workflow tool decision. Without that boundary, the team can keep reopening the temporary decision by debating the larger technology decision.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

The useful closure move is separating the temporary workaround decision from the long-term workflow tool decision. Without that boundary, the team can keep reopening the temporary decision by debating the larger technology decision.

title: What is missing?
body:
A meeting ends with this summary:

“The group discussed the temporary intake workaround and agreed it was broadly sensible. Risk comments were noted. Technology was comfortable as long as this does not become a permanent system. The team will keep progressing.”

What is missing before this decision is ready to move to action?

A. A clear statement of what was decided.

B. Who made the decision.

C. What conditions must be met.

D. Who needs to know.

E. What happens next.

F. A detailed business case for the long-term workflow tool.
instruction: Select the strongest set of missing elements.

_items.text: A, B, C, D and E
A, B and F
C, D, E and F
Only F

_feedback.correct:
Correct. The summary suggests support, but it does not clearly close the decision.

Before the team can act, people need to know what was decided, who decided, what conditions apply, who needs to be informed and what happens next.

The long-term workflow tool may need its own decision process, but it should not keep this temporary workaround decision open.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite. The summary suggests support, but it does not clearly close the decision.

Before the team can act, people need to know what was decided, who decided, what conditions apply, who needs to be informed and what happens next.

The long-term workflow tool may need its own decision process, but it should not keep this temporary workaround decision open.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The summary suggests support, but it does not clearly close the decision.

Before the team can act, people need to know what was decided, who decided, what conditions apply, who needs to be informed and what happens next.

The long-term workflow tool may need its own decision process, but it should not keep this temporary workaround decision open.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite. The summary suggests support, but it does not clearly close the decision.

Before the team can act, people need to know what was decided, who decided, what conditions apply, who needs to be informed and what happens next.

The long-term workflow tool may need its own decision process, but it should not keep this temporary workaround decision open.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The summary suggests support, but it does not clearly close the decision.

Before the team can act, people need to know what was decided, who decided, what conditions apply, who needs to be informed and what happens next.

The long-term workflow tool may need its own decision process, but it should not keep this temporary workaround decision open.

title: Quick check and transition
body: Decision closure creates a clear reference point for action.
instruction: Which statement best describes decision closure?

_items.text: Closure means everyone agrees with the decision.
Closure means the decision is final and can never be revisited.
Closure means what was decided, by whom, why, what remains open and what happens next are clear.
Closure means the decision has been mentioned in meeting notes.

_feedback.correct:
That is the best answer.

Decision closure creates a clear reference point for action. It confirms what was decided, who decided, why, what remains open, and what happens next.

Once a decision is closed, the work is not finished.

A closed decision still needs to move into action.

The next section introduces Move it forward: how to turn the decision into follow-through, feedback and learning.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

Closure is not the same as universal agreement, permanent certainty or a vague note in the minutes.

It is the discipline of making the decision clear enough to act on and stable enough to avoid casual reopening.

Once a decision is closed, the work is not finished.

A closed decision still needs to move into action.

The next section introduces Move it forward: how to turn the decision into follow-through, feedback and learning.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Closure is not the same as universal agreement, permanent certainty or a vague note in the minutes.

It is the discipline of making the decision clear enough to act on and stable enough to avoid casual reopening.

Once a decision is closed, the work is not finished.

A closed decision still needs to move into action.

The next section introduces Move it forward: how to turn the decision into follow-through, feedback and learning.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

Closure is not the same as universal agreement, permanent certainty or a vague note in the minutes.

It is the discipline of making the decision clear enough to act on and stable enough to avoid casual reopening.

Once a decision is closed, the work is not finished.

A closed decision still needs to move into action.

The next section introduces Move it forward: how to turn the decision into follow-through, feedback and learning.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Closure is not the same as universal agreement, permanent certainty or a vague note in the minutes.

It is the discipline of making the decision clear enough to act on and stable enough to avoid casual reopening.

Once a decision is closed, the work is not finished.

A closed decision still needs to move into action.

The next section introduces Move it forward: how to turn the decision into follow-through, feedback and learning.

title: Closed is not complete
body:
Once a decision is closed, the work is not finished.

A closed decision still needs to move into action.

If ownership, readiness or follow-through is unclear, the decision can stall after it was supposedly made.

That is the purpose of Move it forward.

title: The decision-execution gap
body:
Many decisions do not fail at the moment of decision.

They fail in the handover.

They fail when nobody owns the next step.

They fail when dependencies are assumed.

They fail when affected people are told too late.

They fail when early warning signs are invisible.

They fail when learning only happens after something has already gone properly sideways.

A decision that does not move into action is not really a decision.

It is a well-dressed intention.

title: What helps a decision move?
body:
Moving a decision forward means making the next movement clear.

A useful follow-through check answers six practical questions.

1

Action owner

Who owns implementation or follow-through?

2

First actions

What needs to happen first?

3

Readiness

What needs to be true for this to work?

4

Dependencies

Who or what else could slow it down?

5

Feedback

What early signals will tell us whether it is working?

6

Learning

What will we review so future decisions get faster and better?

A closed decision still needs movement.

A decision moves forward when people know what happens next, who owns the action, what needs to be ready, what feedback matters, and when the decision may need to be adjusted.

title: Do not turn every decision into a project
displayTitle: Do not turn every decision into a project
body:
Move it forward does not mean turning every decision into a full project plan.

The level of follow-through should fit the decision.

Light follow-through

For simple or local decisions:

  • one owner;
  • one next action;
  • one communication;
  • simple follow-up.

More deliberate follow-through

For complex, cross-functional or higher-risk decisions:

  • implementation ownership;
  • readiness checks;
  • dependency management;
  • communication plan;
  • feedback points;
  • review and learning.

The point is not more governance.

The point is enough follow-through to stop the decision disappearing after closure.

title: Back to The Request Queue
body:
In The Request Queue, closing the decision would not be enough.

The team would still need to move the temporary workaround into action.

That means making the first steps clear.

Example follow-through plan

The temporary workaround will start next Monday.

The team lead owns implementation for the trial.

The first actions are:

  • set up the shared inbox;
  • finalise the short intake form;
  • create the shared tracker;
  • confirm Risk’s minimum tracker fields;
  • confirm Technology’s boundary conditions;
  • brief affected stakeholders on how urgent requests should be raised;
  • run a daily 15-minute triage for the first two weeks;
  • review after four weeks.

The trial will be adjusted if urgent requests are delayed, control conditions are not being met, or the triage process creates unsustainable workload.

title: What would help this move?
body:
Which actions would most help the temporary workaround move into practice?

A. Set up the shared inbox before the launch date.

B. Confirm who owns implementation of the trial.

C. Tell stakeholders the old request pathways will continue as usual.

D. Define what early signals would trigger an adjustment or escalation.

E. Wait until the full workflow tool is ready.

F. Brief affected stakeholders on how urgent requests should be raised during the trial.
instruction: Select the strongest set of actions.

_items.text: A, B, D and F
A, C, D and E
B, C, E and F
A, B, C, D, E and F

_feedback.correct:
Correct. The strongest actions are the ones that make movement visible: setting up the mechanism, naming implementation ownership, defining early signals and briefing affected people.

Continuing the old request pathways would undermine the trial.

Waiting for the full workflow tool avoids the temporary decision rather than moving it forward.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, B, D and F because they make movement visible: setting up the mechanism, naming implementation ownership, defining early signals and briefing affected people.

Continuing the old request pathways would undermine the trial.

Waiting for the full workflow tool avoids the temporary decision rather than moving it forward.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, B, D and F because they make movement visible: setting up the mechanism, naming implementation ownership, defining early signals and briefing affected people.

Continuing the old request pathways would undermine the trial.

Waiting for the full workflow tool avoids the temporary decision rather than moving it forward.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, B, D and F because they make movement visible: setting up the mechanism, naming implementation ownership, defining early signals and briefing affected people.

Continuing the old request pathways would undermine the trial.

Waiting for the full workflow tool avoids the temporary decision rather than moving it forward.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, B, D and F because they make movement visible: setting up the mechanism, naming implementation ownership, defining early signals and briefing affected people.

Continuing the old request pathways would undermine the trial.

Waiting for the full workflow tool avoids the temporary decision rather than moving it forward.

title: Learning protects future velocity
body:
Moving a decision forward also means learning from what happens next.

That does not require a formal review for every decision.

But for material decisions, it is useful to ask:

Did the decision produce the intended effect?

Did the decision process work?

Were the right people involved at the right time?

Did advice, agreement or approval work as expected?

Did the decision close clearly?

Did execution reveal anything we should learn for next time?

Learning matters because velocity improves when teams recognise patterns.

They get clearer about what needs rigour, what can be delegated, what advice matters, and where decision drag usually appears.

title: Quick check and transition
body: Move it forward is the discipline of turning a closed decision into action, follow-through and learning at the right level of rigour.
instruction: Which statement best describes Move it forward?

_items.text: It means creating a full project plan for every decision.
It means the decision is complete once it has been announced.
It means turning the decision into action, follow-through and learning at the right level of rigour.
It means the Performer becomes responsible for the original decision.

_feedback.correct:
That is the best answer.

Move it forward means making sure a closed decision becomes action. It clarifies ownership, first steps, readiness, feedback and learning without over-engineering every decision.

You have now seen the five stages of Velocity:

1. Frame the decision
2. Define the roles
3. Build confidence
4. Close the decision
5. Move it forward

The next section brings the method together in an integrated practice scenario.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

Move it forward is not about creating heavy project governance or handing the whole decision to the Performer. It is about making the next movement clear enough that the decision does not disappear after closure.

You have now seen the five stages of Velocity:

1. Frame the decision
2. Define the roles
3. Build confidence
4. Close the decision
5. Move it forward

The next section brings the method together in an integrated practice scenario.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Move it forward is not about creating heavy project governance or handing the whole decision to the Performer. It is about making the next movement clear enough that the decision does not disappear after closure.

You have now seen the five stages of Velocity:

1. Frame the decision
2. Define the roles
3. Build confidence
4. Close the decision
5. Move it forward

The next section brings the method together in an integrated practice scenario.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

Move it forward is not about creating heavy project governance or handing the whole decision to the Performer. It is about making the next movement clear enough that the decision does not disappear after closure.

You have now seen the five stages of Velocity:

1. Frame the decision
2. Define the roles
3. Build confidence
4. Close the decision
5. Move it forward

The next section brings the method together in an integrated practice scenario.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

Move it forward is not about creating heavy project governance or handing the whole decision to the Performer. It is about making the next movement clear enough that the decision does not disappear after closure.

You have now seen the five stages of Velocity:

1. Frame the decision
2. Define the roles
3. Build confidence
4. Close the decision
5. Move it forward

The next section brings the method together in an integrated practice scenario.

title: Put the method to work
body:
You have now seen the five stages of Velocity:

  1. Frame the decision
  2. Define the roles
  3. Build confidence
  4. Close the decision
  5. Move it forward

Now you will use the method on a new workplace scenario.

The aim is not to find a perfect answer.

The aim is to work out what the decision needs next.

title: Scenario: The priority request rule
body:
The priority request rule

A support team manages requests from several business areas.

Recently, more requests have been marked as urgent.

Some are genuinely time-critical. Others are urgent because someone senior asked for them. Others are urgent because the request was submitted late.

The result is predictable: urgent work keeps jumping the queue, planned work is delayed, and the team is spending more time negotiating priorities than completing work.

The team lead proposes a simple priority rule for the next six weeks:

  • urgent requests must include a reason;
  • requests linked to regulatory, client, financial or operational risk are prioritised first;
  • late requests are not automatically urgent;
  • disputed priorities go to a short daily triage;
  • the rule is reviewed after six weeks.

Most people agree the current situation is not working.

But the proposal has started to drift.

Business stakeholders

Want flexibility for senior requests.

Risk

Wants assurance that genuine control or client-impact issues are not delayed.

The support team

Wants fewer priority fights.

The team lead’s manager

Wants the rule to reduce noise without creating another layer of process.

No one is blocking the proposal.

But no one is quite sure whether it is ready to decide.

title: The messy decision note
body:
Here is the current decision note.

“The team discussed the proposed urgent request rule and agreed it makes sense in principle.

Stakeholders want to make sure urgent business needs are not slowed down. Risk wants genuine control issues prioritised. The support team wants clearer rules and fewer interruptions.

The team will keep refining the proposal and make sure the right people are comfortable before changing the process.”
instruction: What is the main problem with this note?

_items.text: It is too short.
It does not show that enough stakeholders were consulted.
It sounds aligned, but the decision is not framed, owned, closed or ready to move.
It should be escalated immediately.

_feedback.correct:
That is the strongest answer.

The note creates a sense of progress, but it does not clearly say what decision is being made, who decides, what conditions must be resolved, what has been closed or what happens next.

That is exactly where Velocity helps.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

The issue is not simply length, consultation or escalation. The note sounds constructive, but it does not create enough clarity for the decision to move.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

The issue is not simply length, consultation or escalation. The note sounds constructive, but it does not create enough clarity for the decision to move.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

The issue is not simply length, consultation or escalation. The note sounds constructive, but it does not create enough clarity for the decision to move.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

The issue is not simply length, consultation or escalation. The note sounds constructive, but it does not create enough clarity for the decision to move.

title: Step 1: Frame the decision
body: A useful frame makes the decision specific enough for people to know what they are contributing to.
instruction: Which frame would most help this decision move?

_items.text: “How do we stop people marking everything as urgent?”
“Should we trial a priority rule for urgent requests for six weeks, using agreed criteria and a daily triage for disputed cases?”
“How do we make stakeholders follow the process?”
“Should all business stakeholders agree before the new rule starts?”

_feedback.correct:
That is the strongest frame.

It clarifies the choice, the trial period, the proposed mechanism and the boundary.

It does not try to solve every priority issue forever.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

A strong frame is specific enough to guide roles, input, confidence-building and closure. Broad frustration is not yet a decision.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

A strong frame is specific enough to guide roles, input, confidence-building and closure. Broad frustration is not yet a decision.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

A strong frame is specific enough to guide roles, input, confidence-building and closure. Broad frustration is not yet a decision.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

A strong frame is specific enough to guide roles, input, confidence-building and closure. Broad frustration is not yet a decision.

title: Step 2: Define the roles
body: Once the decision is framed, the next question is who needs to play which role.
instruction: What role issue most needs to be clarified?

_items.text: Whether every stakeholder who submits requests has approval rights over the rule.
Who recommends the rule, who decides whether to trial it, who provides input, and whether Risk has any required agreement conditions.
Whether the support team should perform all urgent requests without challenge.
Whether Risk should decide all priority requests.

_feedback.correct:
That is the strongest answer.

The decision needs clear roles. Business stakeholders may provide input. Risk may provide advice or required agreement conditions. The support team may perform the process. One person or forum still needs to decide whether the trial proceeds.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite.

The issue is not solved by giving everyone approval rights or handing the whole decision to one stakeholder group. The roles need to distinguish input, agreement, decision authority and performance.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

The issue is not solved by giving everyone approval rights or handing the whole decision to one stakeholder group. The roles need to distinguish input, agreement, decision authority and performance.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite.

The issue is not solved by giving everyone approval rights or handing the whole decision to one stakeholder group. The roles need to distinguish input, agreement, decision authority and performance.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite.

The issue is not solved by giving everyone approval rights or handing the whole decision to one stakeholder group. The roles need to distinguish input, agreement, decision authority and performance.

title: Step 3: Build confidence
body:
Build confidence means resolving what matters enough to decide responsibly.

It does not mean removing all uncertainty.

Which actions would most help the team build enough confidence to decide?

A. Ask Risk which types of request must always be treated as urgent because of control, client or regulatory impact.

B. Ask every business stakeholder to approve the wording of the priority rule.

C. Review recent urgent requests to understand which ones were genuinely time-critical.

D. Ask the support team what operational impact the daily triage would create.

E. Delay the decision until every possible priority scenario has been documented.

F. Run a short challenge discussion: what could make the rule fail in the first six weeks?
instruction: Select the strongest set of actions.

_items.text: A, C, D and F
A, B, C and E
B, D, E and F
A, B, C, D, E and F

_feedback.correct:
Correct. The strongest actions answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, evidence from recent requests, operational feasibility and likely failure points.

Broad approval from every stakeholder may not be necessary unless they have a defined agreement role.

Waiting for every possible scenario would create delay without necessarily improving the decision.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, C, D and F because they answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, evidence from recent requests, operational feasibility and likely failure points.

Broad approval from every stakeholder may not be necessary unless they have a defined agreement role.

Waiting for every possible scenario would create delay without necessarily improving the decision.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, C, D and F because they answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, evidence from recent requests, operational feasibility and likely failure points.

Broad approval from every stakeholder may not be necessary unless they have a defined agreement role.

Waiting for every possible scenario would create delay without necessarily improving the decision.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, C, D and F because they answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, evidence from recent requests, operational feasibility and likely failure points.

Broad approval from every stakeholder may not be necessary unless they have a defined agreement role.

Waiting for every possible scenario would create delay without necessarily improving the decision.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, C, D and F because they answer decision-critical questions: risk conditions, evidence from recent requests, operational feasibility and likely failure points.

Broad approval from every stakeholder may not be necessary unless they have a defined agreement role.

Waiting for every possible scenario would create delay without necessarily improving the decision.

title: Step 4: Close the decision
body:
If the team decides to trial the rule, the decision needs to be closed clearly.

Which elements should be included in a clear closure statement?

A. What was decided.

B. Who made the decision.

C. Why the trial is being run.

D. The criteria for urgent requests.

E. Who needs to be informed.

F. A promise that no disputed requests will happen.
