G. What would trigger review, adjustment or escalation.
instruction: Select the strongest set of closure elements.

_items.text: A, B, C, D, E and G
A, C, F and G
B, D, E and F
Only F

_feedback.correct:
Correct. A clear closure statement should confirm what was decided, who decided, why, what boundaries or criteria apply, who needs to know and what would trigger review or adjustment.

A promise that no disputed requests will happen is not realistic. A good decision makes disputes easier to handle; it does not pretend they will disappear.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite. A clear closure statement should confirm what was decided, who decided, why, what boundaries or criteria apply, who needs to know and what would trigger review or adjustment.

A promise that no disputed requests will happen is not realistic. A good decision makes disputes easier to handle; it does not pretend they will disappear.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. A clear closure statement should confirm what was decided, who decided, why, what boundaries or criteria apply, who needs to know and what would trigger review or adjustment.

A promise that no disputed requests will happen is not realistic. A good decision makes disputes easier to handle; it does not pretend they will disappear.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite. A clear closure statement should confirm what was decided, who decided, why, what boundaries or criteria apply, who needs to know and what would trigger review or adjustment.

A promise that no disputed requests will happen is not realistic. A good decision makes disputes easier to handle; it does not pretend they will disappear.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. A clear closure statement should confirm what was decided, who decided, why, what boundaries or criteria apply, who needs to know and what would trigger review or adjustment.

A promise that no disputed requests will happen is not realistic. A good decision makes disputes easier to handle; it does not pretend they will disappear.

title: Step 5: Move it forward
body:
A closed decision still needs movement.

The trial will only work if people know what happens next.

Which actions would most help the priority rule move into practice?

A. Confirm who owns the six-week trial.

B. Update the request instructions with the urgent request criteria.

C. Continue accepting urgent requests through informal side channels.

D. Track how many requests are marked urgent during the trial.

E. Review the rule after six weeks and decide whether to continue, adjust or stop.

F. Wait for stakeholders to complain before reviewing the process.
instruction: Select the strongest set of actions.

_items.text: A, B, D and E
A, C, D and F
B, C, E and F
A, B, C, D, E and F

_feedback.correct:
Correct. The strongest actions make follow-through visible: ownership, updated instructions, feedback and review.

Keeping informal side channels would undermine the trial.

Waiting for complaints is not a feedback loop. It is just delayed awareness wearing a suit.

_incorrect.final:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, B, D and E because they make follow-through visible: ownership, updated instructions, feedback and review.

Keeping informal side channels would undermine the trial.

Waiting for complaints is not a feedback loop. It is just delayed awareness wearing a suit.
_incorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, B, D and E because they make follow-through visible: ownership, updated instructions, feedback and review.

Keeping informal side channels would undermine the trial.

Waiting for complaints is not a feedback loop. It is just delayed awareness wearing a suit.

_partlyCorrect.final:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, B, D and E because they make follow-through visible: ownership, updated instructions, feedback and review.

Keeping informal side channels would undermine the trial.

Waiting for complaints is not a feedback loop. It is just delayed awareness wearing a suit.
_partlyCorrect.notFinal:
Not quite. The strongest actions are A, B, D and E because they make follow-through visible: ownership, updated instructions, feedback and review.

Keeping informal side channels would undermine the trial.

Waiting for complaints is not a feedback loop. It is just delayed awareness wearing a suit.

title: Model response
body: Here is how Velocity helps this decision move.

_items.title: 1. Frame the decision
_items.body:
The decision is whether to trial a priority rule for urgent requests for six weeks.

This separates the immediate process decision from larger questions about resourcing, service levels or the whole request model.

_items.title: 2. Define the roles
_items.body:
The team lead may recommend the rule and own the process.

The team lead’s manager or relevant sponsor may decide whether the trial proceeds.

Business stakeholders provide input on practical impacts.

Risk provides advice or required agreement conditions for control, client or regulatory priority issues.

The support team performs the process.

Affected stakeholders need to be informed about how urgent requests will now be raised.

_items.title: 3. Build confidence
_items.body:
The team needs enough confidence that the rule is workable, does not delay genuinely urgent work, reduces priority noise and can be trialled without creating unnecessary process.

Useful confidence-building includes recent request evidence, Risk advice, operational input and a short challenge discussion.

_items.title: 4. Close the decision
_items.body: The closure statement should confirm what was decided, who decided, why, what counts as urgent, what remains open, who needs to know and when the trial will be reviewed.

_items.title: 5. Move it forward
_items.body: The decision moves when the request instructions are updated, the trial owner is named, urgent request volumes are tracked, and the rule is reviewed after six weeks.

title: Transition to workshop preparation
body:
Velocity helps by turning a broad frustration into a decision that can actually move.

In the workshop, you will use this same method on real decisions.

You will practise framing the decision, defining roles, building confidence, closing clearly and identifying what needs to happen next.

Before then, think of one decision where Velocity could help.

It might be slow, unclear, over-consulted, repeatedly escalated, hard to close, or difficult to move into action.

You do not need to solve it now.

Just bring it to mind.

title: From method to application
body:
You have now worked through the five stages of Velocity:

  1. Frame the decision
  2. Define the roles
  3. Build confidence
  4. Close the decision
  5. Move it forward

The next step is applying the method to real decisions.

That is the purpose of the Applied Velocity Workshop.

title: What happens in the workshop
body:
In the Applied Velocity Workshop, you will use the Velocity method to work through real decisions or decision challenges.

The workshop will help you:

Frame the decision clearly

Define who needs to play which role

Identify what advice, evidence, agreement or challenge is needed

Clarify what would allow the decision to close

Identify what needs to happen next

The workshop is not designed to re-teach the whole method.

It is designed to help you apply it.

title: Choose a decision to bring
body:
Before the workshop, think of one decision where Velocity could help.

It might be a decision that is:

Slow

Unclear

Over-consulted

Repeatedly escalated

Hard to close

Difficult to move into action

It does not need to be dramatic.

Often the most useful examples are ordinary decisions that keep taking more effort than they should.

title: What makes a decision suitable?
body:
A useful workshop decision is specific enough to work with.

More useful

“Should we trial a new intake process for this team?”

Less useful

“How do we improve collaboration?”

More useful

“Who should decide whether this workaround proceeds?”

Less useful

“Why are decisions slow here?”

More useful

“What input do we need before closing this change?”

Less useful

“How do we get everyone aligned?”

More useful

“How do we move this agreed decision into action?”

Less useful

“How do we fix the whole operating model?”

If the decision is too broad, the first workshop task will be to frame it.

That is fine. Just bring the real issue.

title: Bring one decision to mind
body:
Think of one decision or decision challenge you could bring to the workshop.

Use these prompts privately. You do not need to enter the details into this module.

Private reflection prompts

  • What decision is being made?
  • Why does it matter?
  • Where does it feel stuck?
  • Who seems to be involved?
  • What would improve if the decision moved better?

You do not need to solve it now.

Just bring it to mind.

Privacy note: This module does not ask you to submit a real decision example. Bring the example to the workshop instead.

title: What to bring, what not to worry about
displayTitle: What to bring, what not to worry about
body:
Bring

  • one real decision or decision challenge;
  • enough context to explain why it matters;
  • a sense of where it feels stuck;
  • any known constraints, roles, risks or timing pressures.

Do not worry about

  • a finished solution;
  • a perfect decision brief;
  • full stakeholder agreement;
  • every piece of evidence;
  • a polished presentation.

The workshop will help structure the decision.

title: Final summary
body:
You have completed the introduction to Velocity.

You have seen how slow decisions become normal, and how the five-stage method helps decisions move:

  1. Frame the decision
  2. Define the roles
  3. Build confidence
  4. Close the decision
  5. Move it forward

In the workshop, you will use this method on real work.

Bring one decision to mind, and be ready to look at it through the Velocity stages.

You are ready for the Applied Velocity Workshop.
